Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-010 — Tripod Vessel with Ge Yao Glaze

Reference Object ro-song-010

Tripod Vessel with Ge Yao Glaze

哥窑 — Southern Song – Yuan Dynasty, 12th–14th century

Song Dynasty Yuan Dynasty Ge Ware Guan Tradition Crackle Glaze GOM Examined

Object Description

A tripod censer of compact circular form, supported on three bulbous feet and fitted with three small loop handles at the rim. The body is covered overall with a thick, opaque Ge-type glaze of warm ivory-grey tone. The entire exterior surface, including feet and handles, carries a dense and well-preserved crackle network of exceptional clarity.

The glaze exhibits the characteristic jade-like, non-vitreous optical quality associated with authentic Southern Song Guan and Ge wares, produced by a dense population of enclosed microbubbles within the glaze matrix. Glaze application is even and controlled throughout; no pooling or drips are present.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The object was examined using a non-destructive Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) protocol at magnifications from 50× to 200×. Observations document glaze microstructure, crack architecture, surface microtexture, firing traces, and long-term ageing. Results were compared with published SEM–EDS, XRF, and XRD studies of authenticated Southern Song Guan and Ge tradition wares.

Gemmological Optical Microscopy — GOM Documentation

~100×

Fig. 4. Granular glaze surface microtexture. Primary crack channels visible with iron-manganese patination.

~150×

Fig. 5. Kiln spur contact zone. Vitrified central area with iron-enriched peripheral halo. No secondary alteration.

~200×

Fig. 6. Dense sealed microbubble population (50–300 μm). Homogeneous distribution; partial collapse at surface boundary.

1. Double Crackle Morphology — 双线开片

GOM examination reveals two structurally independent crack systems at different optical depths. The primary network forms large polygonal cells with dark brown to near-black iron-rich patination in deep channels. The secondary network subdivides these primary fields into finer, shallower units carrying lighter reddish oxidation products. This hierarchical bipartite architecture corresponds to the classical Golden Thread and Iron Wire (金丝铁线) structure canonical for authenticated Ge ware.

2. Iron–Manganese Diffusion within Crackles

Each crack demonstrates a graduated chromatic transition: dark brown to near-black mineral deposits in deeper zones, reddish-orange oxidation products near the glaze surface, with a continuous colour gradient throughout crack depth. This diffusion profile reflects centuries of natural oxidation and cannot be reproduced by surface application of colourants, which would produce uniform staining without depth differentiation.

3. Glaze Microbubble Structure

At 100×–200×, the glaze matrix displays a dense population of sealed spherical microbubbles (estimated 50–300 μm diameter), distributed homogeneously throughout. These enclosed gas voids represent gases trapped during repeated thick glaze application and high-temperature firing. The dense bubble population acts as a distributed light-scattering medium, producing the characteristic jade-like optical effect of authentic Guan wares.

4. Granular Glaze Microtexture

The glaze surface presents a fine granular microrelief with an extremely fine crystalline texture and subtle sugar-like granulation producing gentle optical scattering. No mechanical polishing marks or modern treatment traces are observed. This microtexture is consistent with slow controlled cooling of thick Song dynasty glazes and is not reproducible by modern rapid-firing schedules.

5. Firing Support Marks

Microscopic examination of the base identifies original kiln support scars: a vitrified central contact zone, an iron-enriched peripheral halo, locally thickened glaze around the scar perimeter, and complete absence of secondary mechanical alteration. These traces correspond to refractory spur supports used in Southern Song imperial kilns.

6. Optical Ageing

Long-term natural ageing is expressed through progressive infilling of crack channels with mineral precipitates, stable crack margins with no fresh fracture surfaces, and integrated weathering across the full glaze area. The ageing pattern is internally consistent throughout the entire object, indicating a unified long-term environmental history.

Diagnostic Interpretation

Authentication is established through the mutual agreement of multiple independent diagnostic indicators. No single feature alone constitutes proof; it is the convergence of all markers that is diagnostically decisive.

The double crackle architecture, natural iron–manganese diffusion profiles, dense sealed microbubble population, granular glaze microtexture, original kiln support scars, and integrated optical ageing form a technologically coherent and mutually reinforcing system. All indicators are consistent with authenticated Southern Song Guan/Ge tradition ceramics.
Diagnostic Feature Category Diagnostic Value
Double crackle architecture (金丝铁线)CrackleVery High
Iron–manganese diffusion profileCrackle / AgeingVery High
Sealed glaze microbubbles (50–300 μm)MicrostructureHigh
Granular glaze microtextureGlaze surfaceHigh
Original kiln support scarsFiring tracesHigh
Integrated natural ageingAgeingHigh

Key Diagnostic Markers

Double crackle architecture — 金丝铁线
Iron–manganese diffusion profile (depth-graduated, naturally formed)
Sealed glaze microbubbles (50–300 μm, homogeneous distribution)
Granular glaze microtexture (consistent with Song slow-cooling technology)
Original kiln support scars (vitrified zone with iron-enriched halo)
Natural long-term glaze ageing (integrated, internally consistent)
High internal diagnostic consistency — multi-category convergence

Diagnostic Confidence: Very High
